results.title: 7 Alternatives to ‘Scaling’ Your Business | Tim Frick
results.content:
# Tim Frick’s Post

Tim Frick

 Report this post

Except for a brief (and ill-fated) growth experiment during the mid-2010s, I've kept my company Mightybytes, Inc. small and manageable for most of our 22 years in business, growing only when it made sense to increase the positive impact we could have on the world. By prioritizing purpose alongside profit and maintaining balance, we offer a viable alternative to the traditional 'scale-and-sell' approach to business. Turns out, there's a whole community of entrepreneurs who share this approach. I talked to a few of them for this article and got some great advice. I hope you find it useful. #business #bcorps #purposedriven #impact #scaling

7 Alternatives to ‘Scaling’ Your Business   bthechange.com

78   5 Comments

Like   Comment [...] Even those who choose less growth can be an inspiration to other entrepreneurs who will take up the flag and advance the impact of sustainable business!  Tim Frick Mightybytes, Inc. #bcorps #purposedriven #impact #scaling #sustainability [...] Tim, a really excellent piece and a must-read for entrepreneurs. You've taken a very clear-eyed look at various growth options and the pitfalls of fast growth - things each of us must consider carefully. I'm philosophically in favor of more growth rather than less, in order to maximize a sustainable company's impact - and the overall sustainable economy. And because the clock is ticking on the endgame of our environmental impact, I'm generally in favor of accelerating sustainable companies' growth to accelerate their positive impact (and crowd out the negative impact of unsustainable companies). But you're absolutely right that every entrepreneur must know their goals clearly and their comfort with the rate of growth they choose. Even those who choose less growth can be an inspiration to

results.title: 5 Scale AI Alternatives [After the Meta Deal] | SuperAnnotate
results.content:
Back to blog

#### Contents

Table of content Item

Earlier this year, Meta’s $15B investment in Scale AI raised new questions about vendor neutrality in model data and evaluation workflows. With Scale now closely tied to Meta, many teams are reassessing how they manage training data, labeling pipelines, and LLM evaluation. The search is shifting toward independent, enterprise-grade platforms that combine secure data infrastructure with expert-led managed services.

This article explores five alternatives to Scale AI – what they offer, how they handle sensitive data, and how they scale labeling and evaluation without conflicts of interest.

## Scale AI [...] ## Scale AI

Scale AI was founded in 2016 by Alexander Wang, with a recent valuation of $29B. Their top customers were OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Department of Defense. Just a week following the Meta investment, two of these major players, OpenAI and Google, pulled back. They announced plans to move away from Scale AI for dataset creation. Scale AI competitors are receiving an influx of interest from AI model providers looking for “neutral” partners.

Scale AI’s main offering is their workforce of freelance, and contracted annotators, managed by engagement leads. [...] Weaknesses

 Not built for full customization across different data types
 Doesn’t focus on multi-team project coordination
 Less flexible for complex or multimodal GenAI projects

## Final Thoughts

Meta’s $15B investment in Scale, and the decision to bring its CEO into the superintelligence lab, has created tension with Scale’s biggest customers. OpenAI and Google are already moving away, and others may not be far behind. When your data infrastructure is tied to a direct competitor, it’s hard to ignore the risks.

If you’re rethinking your setup, this is a good time to explore other options. Whether you need more transparency, flexibility, or simply a neutral partner, there are strong alternatives to consider.

results.title: Get a free tool called Magpie on Github that basically does the same thing :: Lossless Scaling Általános témák
results.content:
My computer is good enough to handle those games at their max on 1080p, but with my multimonitor setup, making those games work at 900p with LS1 or FSR works like magic for multitasking. Everything feels so smooth, with no changes that my eye can see (apart from UI changes). So, with it's only price of 5$ and It's literally free to use with it's demo, this is way better alternative than magpie. [...] Also keep in mind "LS" has an unlimited free demo and is non-profit with proceeds supporting the Ukraine war. It's only $5, has highly active development, a responsive author, and it installs and updates itself automatically through Steam. Just my 2c. [...] use both your screens efficiently, display unsupported resolutions, or enlarge a small unreadable window?  
  
For me it's this latter scenario, even if it's still just to run a silly game. For me the silly game is one I'm quite invested in; 'Lossless Scaling' ("LS") makes it way easier and more enjoyable to play, so for me it's easily worth way more than $5.  
  
Before I had 'IntegerScaler' (which I've replaced with "LS") my accessibility solution and recommendation to others was to install Windows in a VM and run the game inside that, which took hours. And THAT was a paradigm-shifting solution that people searched for for years, lol. Magpie is easier than that, but still way out of reach for most.

results.title: Transforming and scaling data
results.content:
There are many other scaling procedures which can be useful in different situations. You can explore scaling on this very good Wikipedia page or in this article at Towards Data Science which both mention many other different procedures such as the robust scaler and unit vector scaler.

Another important thing to point out is that the terminology used in scaling and transformations can get very confusing and unclear, with each article refering to a single procedure with a different name and sometimes even using one name for two different concepts. Unfortunately, there is no way to avoid this issue. Probably the best strategy to avoid confusion around scaling is to remember the concepts and formulas instead of names and present those in your reports to be as clear as possible. [...] # 3. Part II: Scaling

 Img 

Credits: Hans-Petter Fjeld (CC BY-SA)

Scaling describes a set of procedures used to adjust the distribution of data, particularly the range, through linear transformations. Linear transformation in this context means that it uses only basic arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) and not exponentiating or logarithms. [...] As you can see there are plenty of reasons why we would want to use normalization rather than another type of scaling or transformation. Since showing all of these options is beyond the scope of this tutorial, we will only learn how to apply the normalization procedure in R and show its effects through histograms.

For this part we will work with a different but a very well known dataset called Palmer Penguins. It is available through a package in R so you just need to install it and you can access the data at any point hereafter by loading the library.

 Img 

Source: Palmer Penguins R package vignette. Artwork by Allison Horst.

results.title: Business Scaling vs Business Growth: What Entrepreneurs Should Know
results.content:
Opening multiple offices or new locations is more often a growth move than pure scaling, because it usually requires significant new resources like office space, staff, and local marketing. It becomes an example of scaling only when the underlying business model and systems are efficient enough that each new office can generate more revenue than it costs to operate, improving the company's overall profit margins rather than just increasing revenue. [...] ## What Is Business Scaling?

Scaling is the version of expansion where revenue moves faster than costs. The defining trait of a scalable business model is operational efficiency that improves as the business gets bigger. A software product that already serves 1,000 customers can usually serve 10,000 with roughly the same infrastructure. A consulting firm that has packaged its methodology into a curriculum can teach 100 cohorts with the same effort it once took to teach one. Whatever the industry, scaling means doing more with the same resources, not doing more by hiring more.

### Key Characteristics of a Scalable Business [...] ## Build the Foundation for Sustainable Growth with InCorp

Growth adds revenue by adding resources. Scaling adds revenue without adding the same resources. Most businesses do both at different points in their lifecycle, and knowing which one the business needs at a given moment is one of the more important entrepreneurial calls. Sustainable growth comes from making that call deliberately rather than by default, and from a business structure that can hold up under either strategy.
